perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, only upon (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; The Discloser can not request it to the Recipient
perturbation.explanation: The change creates an in-text contradiction by altering the conditions under which the Recipient must return or destroy confidential information. Originally, it included the Discloser's request as a trigger, giving the Discloser more control. By removing '(iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient,' and stating 'The Discloser can not request it to the Recipient' the agreement now prevents the Discloser from initiating the return/destruction process, regardless of circumstances. This directly contradicts 3.10, where irreparable damage can be claimed if released to a third party and the discloser has no way to request to return or destroy the copies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement by preventing the Discloser from requesting the return or destruction of confidential information. This conflicts with section 3.10, which states that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if confidential information is disclosed and implies the Discloser's right to seek remedies, including actions to prevent further disclosure, which the change now restricts. Therefore, it's a strong, in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient may disclose the information received to its employees, representatives or agents. The Recipient is not responsible for them to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement, they are only encouraged to do so. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction regarding the Recipient's responsibility for maintaining confidentiality. Originally, the Recipient was obligated to ensure their employees/agents agreed to and upheld the confidentiality terms. The modified text states the Recipient is not responsible for ensuring that their employees/agents keep the information confidential, but the Recipient will still be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein. This means the Recipient is liable for breaches caused by those to whom they disclosed the information, even though they aren't responsible for ensuring those parties keep it confidential, directly contradicting the liability framework established and creating enforcement uncertainty. This is also contradicting with 3.11, where they Recipient needs to provide assistance if there's a breach of confidence, which is contradictory since they don't have obligations on them.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.11 The Recipient shall immediately notify upon becoming aware of any breach of confidence by anybody to whom it has disclosed the Confidential Information and give all necessary assistance in connection with any steps which the Discloser may wish to take prevent, stop or obtain compensation for such a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 3.2 to state that the Recipient is not responsible for ensuring their employees/agents keep the information confidential. This directly contradicts the statement in section 3.11, which states that 'The Recipient shall immediately notify upon becoming aware of any breach of confidence by anybody to whom it has disclosed the Confidential Information and give all necessary assistance'. If the Recipient is not responsible for ensuring confidentiality, it creates a contradiction regarding the Recipient's obligations in case of a breach of confidentiality by those parties, hence creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. The Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall end at the end of this agreement, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by changing the duration of the confidentiality obligations. The original text states the Recipient's duty to hold Confidential Information remains in effect 'indefinitely'. The modified text states that it ends when the agreement ends. This creates an uncertainty about what happens to the disclosed information after the end of the agreement. Is it considered public? Can it be shared? What are the obligations? This undermines the fundamental purpose of the NDA, and this is directly contradicting with section 3.8, where it states that Confidentiality Information or copies thereof shall be subject to an indefinite c o n f i d e n t i a l i t y obligation
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.8 Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient may retain such of its documents as required to comply with mandatory law, provided that such Confidentiality Information or copies thereof shall be subject to an indefinite c o n f i d e n t i a l i t y obligation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text states the confidentiality obligation remains in effect 'indefinitely'. The changed text modifies this to state it ends at the end of the agreement. Section 3.8 states that Confidentiality Information shall be subject to an indefinite confidentiality obligation. This creates a direct contradiction concerning the duration of confidentiality, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.13 The Discloser is not under any obligation under this Agreement to disclose any Confidential Information it chooses not to disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.13 The Discloser is under obligation under this Agreement to disclose any Confidential Information requested by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the Discloser has no obligation to disclose specific Confidential Information. The modification imposes an obligation on the Discloser to provide any Confidential Information requested by the Recipient. This introduces a significant contradiction, as the Discloser now has a duty to potentially reveal sensitive data they would prefer to withhold. This contradicts with section 1.1, where Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser, since the discloser, under this change, doesn't have control over what is and what isn't.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Discloser is not obligated to disclose Confidential Information, whereas the changed text mandates the Discloser must disclose any Confidential Information requested by the Recipient. This directly contradicts section 1.1, which defines Confidential Information as the Discloser's proprietary information. The change effectively removes the Discloser's control over what constitutes Confidential Information, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.2 If the Discloser and Recipient succeed in the call for proposal referred to in clause 2 and sign the corresponding Grant Agreement (GA) and Consortium Agreement (CA), or entered into partnership under any other kind of collaborative agreement (COA) or association agreement (AA), the non-disclosure provisions of the CA, COA and AA shall [supplement or supersede] this Agreement. In the event that non-disclosure provisions are not provided for the said private agreements in equal terms as stated herein, this Agreement shall remain in force until the end of the collaboration undertaken or after [months or years] of its termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.2 If the Discloser and Recipient succeed in the call for proposal referred to in clause 2 and sign the corresponding Grant Agreement (GA) and Consortium Agreement (CA), or entered into partnership under any other kind of collaborative agreement (COA) or association agreement (AA), this agreement remains in force, no matter what happens.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. The original clause allows the NDA to be supplemented or superseded by future agreements (CA, COA, AA) if they are signed. The modification removes this possibility, stating that the NDA will remain in force regardless. This could create conflicts if the future agreements contain conflicting confidentiality clauses, making it unclear which agreement's terms prevail. It is directly contradicting with the intention of the agreement, stated in 2. Purpose of the Disclosure of Confidential Information
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Purpose of the Disclosure of Confidential Information The Discloser and Recipient will enter on discussions regarding future collaboration toward European Funded Project in the field of [….] or [will submit a proposal for a collaborative project in response to the call (identify the call) under (identify the EU funded Programme)] or [will enter into or evaluate alternatives for partnership or collaboration with [name of the Recipient] for the purpose of [identify the undertaking intended to perform or achieve].
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 4.1.2, stating that the agreement remains in force no matter what happens, directly contradicts section 2, which outlines the purpose of the disclosure of confidential information as being related to future collaborations and potential agreements. The original 4.1.2 allowed for the possibility of future agreements (CA, COA, AA) to supplement or supersede the current NDA, aligning with the collaborative purpose stated in section 2. The modified version eliminates this possibility, creating a strong inconsistency.
